transcript.presenting_concern: Behavioral activation and processing avoidance patterns post-assault

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Laura, how are you this week? I know we left off last session with the behavioral activation plan — getting you re-engaged with activities you've been avoiding.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. So. I did some of the things on the list. I went to yoga on Tuesday. I had coffee with my friend Priya on Thursday. I even drove past the restaurant where... where it happened. I didn't go in, but I drove past.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Each of those is significant. The yoga was on your list as a "pleasure" activity. Coffee with Priya was a "connection" activity. And driving past the restaurant — that was on your avoidance hierarchy. Let's start there. What was it like to drive past?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My heart was pounding when I got close. Like my body remembered even though my brain was saying "you're fine, it's just a building." I gripped the steering wheel and I turned my head and looked at it. The sign, the door. And then I was past it and I was okay. Shaky but okay.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You confronted an avoidance behavior that you've been doing for two years — driving blocks out of your way to not pass that location. And when you finally did, what did you learn?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That the building can't hurt me. That it's just a place. The danger was the person, not the restaurant, and he's in prison now. My body treats it like the whole location is radioactive but... it's just a Thai restaurant now. They probably have new staff. Nothing about that building is dangerous.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Your cognitive brain knows that. Your limbic system — the fear center — encoded that location as "where something terrible happened" and it generalizes. It says "this place = danger." By driving past and having nothing bad happen, you're teaching your fear system to update its threat assessment. It's like the interoceptive exposure but for locations.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. I want to... eventually I want to go in. Eat there. Take back the space. But not yet.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Not yet is fine. You just did step one on the hierarchy. We move up gradually. Tell me about yoga — how was that?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Really good actually. I was nervous about being in a room with strangers with my eyes closed — that's been a trigger since the assault. But the teacher has a calm voice and I positioned myself near the door, which helped. And by the end of class I felt... peaceful? For like twenty minutes. Which is the longest I've felt peaceful in two years.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's meaningful. You adapted the situation to work for you — positioning near the door for safety — and you were able to access a state of calm. The fact that peace felt notable tells me how rare that experience has been. What about the connection with Priya?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Priya doesn't know what happened. She's a work friend. So with her I can just be normal Laura. We talked about TV shows and office gossip and for an hour I felt like a regular person, not a trauma survivor. That was its own kind of healing.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Normal interactions where your identity isn't defined by what happened — that's important. You're more than what was done to you. How did your mood track across the week? Did you notice changes on days with planned activities versus days without?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Huge difference. Tuesday and Thursday I'd rate my mood at maybe a 4 or 5 — not great but functional. The days I stayed home and did nothing — Monday, Wednesday — I was at a 2. Lying on the couch, doom-scrolling, sleeping in the afternoon.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That data is classic behavioral activation evidence. Activity lifts mood. Withdrawal drops it. The depression tells you "stay in bed, you can't face the world" — and when you listen, the depression gets worse. When you override it — even partially — the mood improves. It's counterintuitive because you have to do the thing BEFORE you feel better, not wait to feel better before doing it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I get that now. The motivation comes after the action, not before.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Now, I want to also check in on the avoidance patterns beyond the restaurant. You've mentioned several: being alone with men you don't know well, being in enclosed spaces, being out after dark, intimate physical contact. Where are these at this week?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The enclosed spaces thing is manageable — I take the elevator now at work most days, which I didn't do for a year. Men — that's still hard. There's a new guy on my team at work and every time he stands behind my desk to look at my screen I flinch. He probably thinks I'm weird.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The startle response — hypervigilance about men in your space — is one of the hallmark PTSD symptoms. It makes complete sense given what you experienced. Have you thought about whether you'd want to tell your manager? Not about the assault, but that you prefer people not to stand behind you — framing it as a personal space preference?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I could do that. I've thought about getting one of those little mirrors people put on their monitors so they can see behind them. That way I can see if anyone is approaching without having to turn around constantly.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's a great practical solution. Environmental modification that gives you a sense of control without requiring disclosure. Get the mirror. And the after-dark avoidance?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That one's been shrinking. I went to a movie last weekend — 7 PM showing, so I walked to my car in the dark after. I was vigilant but not panicked. I had my keys in my hand, I parked under a light, I scanned the parking lot. But I did it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You went to a movie alone and walked to your car in the dark. Two years ago that would have been unthinkable.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. Two years ago I didn't leave my apartment after 5 PM from November to March. So... progress.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Significant progress. Now let's talk about the piece you've been avoiding in our sessions — intimacy. You mentioned last week that you went on a date and it ended with a kiss and you had a panic response.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Yeah. He's nice. We've been texting for a month. The date was good. Dinner, easy conversation. At the end he kissed me and it was gentle and normal and I... I went somewhere else. Like my body was there but my mind went to survival mode. I pulled away really abruptly and he looked confused and hurt and I couldn't explain.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What you're describing is dissociation triggered by physical intimacy — your brain detected a similarity between the current situation and the trauma and activated a protective response. This is not a failing on your part.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: But it's not fair to him. He didn't do anything wrong. And I WANTED to kiss him. My body just... vetoed it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Your body is trying to protect you. It hasn't fully learned the difference between danger and intimacy yet. This is something we can work on, but it requires going very slowly and possibly communicating with this person about your pace.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I texted him after and said "I had a great time but I need to go really slow with physical stuff — it's not about you, it's my stuff." He said he understood and there's no rush.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's excellent communication and it sounds like he responded with respect. The willingness to date at all — to be vulnerable with someone new — that's an act of courage. The pace is yours to set. We can work on graduated exposure to physical intimacy in our sessions — building your tolerance gradually so your body can learn to distinguish safe touch from danger.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I want to. I don't want what happened to me to mean I never get to have a relationship. He took that night from me — I don't want him to take the rest of my life too.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: He won't. You won't let him. Everything you've done this past year — every exposure, every activity, every time you've pushed against the avoidance — is you reclaiming your life. The restaurant, the yoga, the date, the nighttime movie. You are building a life that trauma doesn't control.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Some days it doesn't feel like enough. But today, talking about it, seeing the list of things I've done... maybe it is. Maybe I'm getting there.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You are getting there. For this week: continue yoga, keep the Priya coffee dates, get the mirror for your desk, and — if it feels right — consider a second date where physical boundaries are explicitly yours to set. And try driving past the restaurant one more time. Repetition builds habituation.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. I can do that. Thank you, Dr. Chen. These sessions are my anchor.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're doing the hard work. I just hold the flashlight. Same time next week.

goals: Behavioral activation and processing avoidance patterns post-assault
